teaching_arc.entry_point: Start from the pain of manually starting many nodes and editing code just to change a setting.
teaching_arc.progression: Introduce launch files and parameters as tools for structure, then show how they fit real workflows.
teaching_arc.destination: Students should finish with a practical reason to use launch and parameters instead of treating them as extra syntax.

audience_level: introductory autonomous mobile robotics students learning ROS 2 through lab-first workflows
topic_goal: Teach students how launch files and parameters help organize ROS 2 systems cleanly and make experiments easier to repeat.
topic_takeaways: Students should understand what a launch file solves that manual terminal commands do not.
Students should understand how parameters change node behavior without editing source code.
Students should be able to explain how launch structure improves repeatability and debugging.
style_notes: Keep the tone hands-on and confidence-building.
Connect every concept to what students will debug in a terminal, simulation, or robot lab.
Use visualization and mental models before deep command details.
Tie each abstraction back to repeatability and easier debugging.
transition_style: Move from the mental model to the concrete lab step students will run next, then close each section by explaining what to verify.

slide_ref.source_title: Launch Files + Parameters (YAML) — The Practical Way

slides.narration_seed: Students will tune SLAM/Nav2 via params and launch files. Today we build that foundation.
slides.transition_from_previous: Open by framing the topic goal: Teach students how launch files and parameters help organize ROS 2 systems cleanly and make experiments easier to repeat.
slides.transition_to_next: Carry this idea forward into Why parameters + launch files matter so students feel the lesson progressing intentionally toward the topic goal.
likely_student_confusion: Students may watch the map update without understanding which signals are driving the update.
slides.explanation_style: Frame Launch Files + Parameters (YAML) — The Practical Way as the question or capability the rest of the topic is going to answer.
slides.story_hint: Keep Launch Files + Parameters (YAML) — The Practical Way connected to the lab workflow students will use later in ROS 2 and mobile robotics.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should understand how Launch Files + Parameters (YAML) — The Practical Way sets up the lesson and why the topic matters.
slides.scene_hint: Use a map, scan, and robot pose overlay so students can connect localization or planning state to what they see on screen.

slides.narration_seed: Keep Why parameters + launch files matter efficient and procedural, highlighting only the information students need to act or debug correctly. Keep the pacing efficient and procedural rather than reading the slide word for word.
must_cover: Parameters = settings (numbers/strings/flags) that change node behavior
Launch = start many nodes + set params + remap topics + namespaces
SLAM/Nav2 are mainly: launch + config YAML (lots of parameters)
Goal: you can reproduce runs reliably (same setup every time)
slides.transition_from_previous: After the concept slide, move into the concrete workflow or command sequence students will actually run.
slides.transition_to_next: After the procedure, remind students what successful output or behavior they should expect next.
likely_student_confusion: Students may copy steps mechanically and miss the verification point that tells them the step actually worked.
slides.explanation_style: Keep Why parameters + launch files matter efficient and procedural, highlighting only the information students need to act or debug correctly.
slides.story_hint: Frame the slide as a lab workflow students will run themselves, including what they should verify before moving on.

slide_ref.source_title: Parameters: what they are (and what they are NOT)

slides.narration_seed: Use Parameters: what they are (and what they are NOT) to preview the route of the lesson, explain how this section fits the overall teaching arc, and tell students what to listen for next.
must_cover: Good for: max_speed, use_sim_time, map_frame, thresholds, gains…
Not good for: high-rate sensor data → that belongs in topics
Parameters can be set at startup (recommended) or runtime (if node allows)
Types: bool, int, float, string, arrays (YAML-typed)
slides.transition_from_previous: Bridge from Why parameters + launch files matter into Parameters: what they are (and what they are NOT) so the lesson feels like one continuous explanation rather than isolated slides.

slides.narration_seed: Turn Quiz: Parameters are stored where? into a short guided practice moment: let students think first, then explain the reasoning in a teacher-like way.
must_cover: In ROS 2, parameters are primarily stored…
ROS 2 parameters are per-node. There is no global ROS 1-style parameter server by default.
Give students a moment to decide before explaining the answer.
slides.transition_from_previous: Turn the previous explanation into a decision on Quiz: Parameters are stored where? and let students test whether they can reason with the idea.
slides.transition_to_next: Use the next slide to generalize the reasoning students just practiced.

slide_ref.source_title: YAML parameter files (the standard way for big configs)

slides.narration_seed: Define YAML parameter files (the standard way for big configs) in plain language, then connect the term to one practical robotics consequence so students do not memorize it in isolation.
must_cover: Why YAML: many parameters → easier than 200 CLI param set commands
Rule: use spaces, not tabs
In ROS 2 YAML, parameters go under the key: ros__parameters
You address parameters by node name (and optional namespace)
slides.transition_from_previous: Use YAML parameter files (the standard way for big configs) to introduce the next key term students need before the lesson can move forward.
